author: @ericvonessex9353
text: Excellent video. I believe the alcohol used is also known as surgical spirit? Am I correct? If so it's easily available from Boots the chemist!

replies.author: @GeoffTV2
replies.text: Some surgical spirits may be IPA based.  The Boots one is Ethanol (90%), Methanol (5%) based, with some nasties to stop people drinking it. Crucially it's also about 2.5% castor oil. So using it neat to clean a surface that you want to stick something to doesn't work because there is an oil residue (found that out the hard way). Not saying it won't work in this recipe because I really don't know but it definitely isn't IPA.

replies.author: @ericvonessex9353
replies.text: @GeoffTV2 Many thanks for your very informative reply, much appreciated.

author: @garethb5729
text:
Luke I have an experiment for you, if I may. 
Mount a magnet on a wall with an aluminium drinks can hanging in front of it. Hang it from conductive cord with good contact. Apply voltage to the cord and a brush under the can for ground. 
The reaction is incredible. 
 Fleming's right hand rule is very much missing on you tube. Analogue visuals at least. 
Wear goggles though please.

author: @htmagic
text: Luke, I'm not surprised. In organic chemistry we learned that 'like dissolves like'. So the organic solvents will lift the organic (carbon black) stains in the sink. And the surfactant and universal solvent (water) will carry them away! Cheers!

replies.author: @mamupelu565
replies.text: and the vinegar and salt will remove rust

author: @kuhrd
text: I wouldn't call it a miracle cleaner but it is inexpensive and effective.  You are mixing a solvent, acid, soap (mostly surfactant), and salt together into a solution.  All of these on their own can work well for cleaning depending on what the soil is that you are trying to remove but by combining them together they take some of the guesswork out on what you need to use to clean something.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Looks pretty good. 

For serious stains of say a sink... I'll just put plug in, followed by a couple lumps sodium hydroxide.  fill sink up with water and leave for oh about 3min, before pulling plug and rising hands, and sink. Gives my drains a decent clean on way through too. 

IMHO best thing ever for cleaning tea stains err encrustations... off mugs...  About a minute to restore to pristine. But really do need to ensure rinsed lots. 

Difficult to beat couple quid for decent size tub.

author: @garbleduser
text: Add peroxide for even more power!

author: @timothygorman7528
text:
Looks like an amazing cleaner!  I'm going to have to mix up a batch.

One word of warning though, for all us "Americans" - ha ha - "Washing Up Liquid" is "Dish Soap."  That being said, make sure the "dish soap" you're using in this mix isn't an "OXY" cleaner, or have added ammonia or bleach in it.  These don't do well with Vinegar!   

The "Washing up liquid" may actually only be acting as a cheap "surfactant" (to make it stick better, as both vinegar and alcohol will just about run off of anything before you get to cleaning it) - if that's the case, laundry detergent (non-oxy) does rather well for this.   Dish soap/washing up liquid (gotta love the Brits, right?) is really good at cutting oils and grease, but so is Laundry detergent and might be a safer bet if you're not sure.  I use a 10% Sodium Hypochlorite liquid mixed with some plain old, cheap laundry detergent and distilled water for "soft washing" my house to clean mildew/algae and it works great.

Luke, I think you're pretty awesome.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Tip: Chip Shop Vinegar (industrial acetic acid which one can buy in 5 litre containers) is cheaper, so is commercial washing up liquid (most people use far more detergent than is necessary). A buffing mop in a drill with valve grinding past is easier still (not too fast otherwise you’ll splatter it everywhere).
